_meta.title: DP III — Bitmask, Digit, Tree, DP on DAGs

questions.question: In bitmask DP for TSP, what does dp[S][i] represent?
options: The shortest tour visiting all cities, starting at i
The shortest path visiting exactly the cities in subset S, ending at city i
The number of paths visiting subset S starting at i
The minimum edge weight in subset S connected to i
questions.explanation: dp[S][i] stores the shortest path that visits every city whose bit is set in S exactly once, with the last visited city being i. The transition adds one new city v at a time.

questions.question: In digit DP, what does the 'tight' flag control?
options: Whether the current prefix is non-decreasing
Whether the number is odd or even
Whether the current prefix still equals N's prefix, limiting the next digit
Whether a non-zero digit has been placed yet
questions.explanation: When tight=1, the current prefix matches N exactly so far, so the next digit is bounded by N's digit at that position. Once a smaller digit is placed, tight becomes 0 and all digits 0-9 are allowed.

questions.question: You need the maximum independent set for every node as root of a tree. Which technique gives O(n) total time?
options: Run tree DP rooted at each node — O(n^2)
Rerooting: one post-order DFS for 'down' values, one pre-order DFS for 'up' values
Bitmask DP over all 2^n subsets of vertices
Apply Dijkstra from each node
questions.explanation: Rerooting computes dp-down in a post-order pass, then propagates dp-up in a pre-order pass. Each edge is crossed exactly twice, yielding O(n) total. Running tree DP at every root would be O(n^2).

questions.question: What guarantees that a DP over vertices processes each vertex after all its predecessors?
options: BFS order from an arbitrary start vertex
Topological order of the DAG
Sorting vertices by degree
Post-order traversal of the DFS tree
questions.explanation: A topological order is a linear ordering where every directed edge u->v places u before v. Processing vertices in this order ensures that when we reach v, all predecessors u have already been computed, so the DP transition is valid.

questions.question: Bitmask DP is often used for problems involving subsets. What is the typical time complexity for the travelling salesman problem using bitmask DP?
options: O(n!)
O(n^2 log n)
questions.explanation: Bitmask DP for TSP uses a bitmask of visited cities and the current city as state. There are 2^n subsets and n possible current cities, giving 2^n * n states. Each state transitions to up to n unvisited cities, yielding O(2^n * n^2) total.
